text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 07 April 2019 

Dr Priya Nair
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Priya Nair
Consultant Neurologist,  

Alexander Reed, DOB: 14-03-2004, Hospital No: KCH872311 NHS No. 9473826150  
Flat 3B, 12 Northcote Street, London, SE11 5PT

Thank you for asking me to review this patient. He attended alone, and the history is primarily patient-reported. He is a university student approaching end-of-term examinations and is currently undertaking long study periods with variable sleep. He describes recurrent events over the past two years that he identifies as seizures. He reports an abrupt onset of unresponsiveness witnessed by peers on two occasions, with brief stiffening and a subsequent period of confusion lasting 20–30 minutes. He cannot provide a clear prodrome. There is no consistent daytime pattern other than occurring after consecutive nights of curtailed sleep when preparing for assessments. He emphasised that the episodes occur “Only with sleep deprivation,” and states he has not had events during periods of regular sleep. He denies tongue biting or urinary incontinence to his knowledge. There is no reliable nocturnal history available, as he lives in shared accommodation without regular observation at night.

There is no known family history of seizures and no history of significant head injury, CNS infection, or alcohol misuse. He drinks minimal caffeine but has increased this around exams. He has not been driving. He has never been admitted to hospital for these episodes. He is not currently on anti-seizure medication and has not previously undergone prolonged video-EEG monitoring. A routine EEG one year ago at another centre was reportedly “non-diagnostic” per his account; I do not yet have the formal report. He has not had recent blood tests or neuroimaging that we can verify in our records.

On examination today, general and neurological examinations were unremarkable. We discussed the uncertainty regarding the nature of his events given the limited collateral history and the absence of contemporaneous documentation. Given the temporal link he reports to reduced sleep and his statement that events occur “Only with sleep deprivation,” I have advised careful documentation of sleep patterns and episodes, and I have asked him to seek witness accounts if further events occur. I will request prior EEG results and any imaging from the external provider for review.

I have arranged an ambulatory EEG with sleep capture if feasible, and we will consider inpatient scalp telemetry if non-invasive studies remain inconclusive and if events persist, particularly during the exam period when sleep restriction may recur. We also discussed general safety around study schedules and exam planning. He is aware that the current assessment relies heavily on his own report and that our diagnostic position may evolve as further objective data become available. 

I will update you once the prior investigations are obtained and the ambulatory EEG has been completed. Please let me know if you receive any additional collateral information from family or university welfare services that could clarify the semiology or frequency.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: He describes recurrent events over the past two years that he identifies as seizures.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes "recurrent events over the past two years" and mentions two witnessed episodes with abrupt unresponsiveness, but it does not quantify how often they occur. There is no explicit numeric frequency (per day/week/month/year), no count over a known time period suitable to calculate a rate, and no statement of being seizure-free for at least 6 months. Therefore, the specific seizure frequency cannot be determined from the provided information.
seizure_frequency_number: He describes recurrent events over the past two years that he identifies as seizures.
